items.claim_a: Plato established the Academy in approximately 383 BCE, nearly sixteen years after Socrates' execution [in 399 BCE].
items.claim_b: The Broad Scan does not give a founding date for the Academy, but states Aristotle arrived in 367 BCE and remained for 'approximately twenty years (367–347 BCE).' The Perplexity dossier itself also states the Academy was founded 'around 383 BCE' in one place.

items.claim_a: Perplexity: 'The destruction of the Academy's physical plant occurred during the Mithridatic War in 88 BCE, when the Roman general Sulla's forces destroyed the grounds as part of the siege of Athens.'
items.claim_b: Broad Scan: 'The Academy lasted until the sixth century CE, longer than any comparable institution in European history to that point' (this claim appears in the Perplexity dossier itself at a different point, creating an internal tension, but the Broad Scan states the Academy transformed into 'an Academic school of thought' with no physical destruction date given). Note: The Perplexity dossier itself contradicts itself — it states the Academy 'lasted until the sixth century CE' in one section and that its physical plant was destroyed in '88 BCE' in another section.

items.claim_a: Perplexity: Plato 'died in the year 348 or 347 BCE and was buried in his garden at the Academy in Athens.'
items.claim_b: Broad Scan: Diogenes Laertius reports 'the contents of Plato's will,' detailing 'the disposition of his property, including the Academy grounds,' implying the Academy was institutional property, not a personal garden. The burial location 'in his garden at the Academy' is not directly contradicted but the framing of the Academy as personal property versus institutional property differs.

items.claim_a: Perplexity: Plato's nickname origin — the dossier does not mention the nickname 'Plato' or that his birth name was Aristocles.
items.claim_b: Broad Scan: 'His birth name was likely Aristocles, after his grandfather. "Platon" (meaning "broad" or "wide") was a nickname... from his wrestling coach because of his broad shoulders and chest.' Both sources cite Diogenes Laertius but the Perplexity dossier omits this entirely, referring to him only as 'Plato of Athens' without noting the nickname origin.
